content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


October 18, 2001


Terri Smooke
Special Assistant to the Governor
Office of the Governor
300 South Spring Street, 16th Floor
South Tower
Los Angeles, CA 90013

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-192

Dear Ms. Smooke:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter should not be construed as advice on any conduct that may have already taken place. Finally, our response is based on the facts presented. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in its advice-giving capacity. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Does ownership of shares of stock in Enron Corporation represent a violation of the state's conflict of interest laws?

CONCLUSION

No. Nothing in the Act prohibits the ownership of stock in any company. Moreover, you do not have an economic interest in Enron Corporation.


_______________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-01-192
Page No. 2

FACTS

You serve as special assistant to the Governor. Your responsibilities are serving
as liaison to the Jewish community in the state; serving as liaison with the counsels
general of Israel, speaking to different organizations in California for the Governor and
advising the Governor on issues of concern relevant to the Jewish community. You have
absolutely no role relating to energy issues, and your position does not involve making
policy decisions.

The equity in question, Enron Corporation, is part of a group portfolio. You
belong to a women's investment group of 25 members. The group purchased 150 shares
of Enron Corporation on June 28, 2000, of which you are a 25th owner. The value of the
stock is currently $6,813.00, of which your interest is approximately $272.00.

You have not had any dealings with the company, any of its personnel, its
legislative and regulatory dealings with the Governor's office, nor any knowledge of
energy purchases or contracts.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act nowhere prohibits ownership of any given stock or
other investment interest. Rather, the Act states in Government Code section 87100:

"No public official at any level of state or local government shall
make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official
position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or
has reason to know he has a financial interest."

Thus, the mere fact alone of ownership of Enron stock does not give rise to a conflict of
interest for you.

Instead, potential conflicts of interest arise where there is a confluence of an
economic interest with a governmental decision. To implement section 87100, the
Commission has adopted an eight-step analysis to determine whether a public official
may have a conflict of interest. (Reg. 18700, copy enclosed.)² One of the elements in
determining the presence or absence of a conflict of interest is the identification of a
governmental decision in which the official will make, participate in or influence. (Regs.

² Step One asks whether the individual is a public official. Step Two asks whether the official
will be making, participating in making or using or attempting to use his or her position to influence a
decision. Step Three identifies the public official's economic interests. Step Four determines whether the
given interest identified in Step Three is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision. Steps
Five and Six apply the materiality standard appropriate for the given type of economic interest at issue and
determines whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a material
financial effect on the economic interest. (Reg. 18700.) Steps Seven and Eight are exceptions to the
conflicts provisions, to be considered in the event a conflict of interest arises.

File No. A-01-192
Page No. 3

18700 - 18708.) In most circumstances, because you have not identified any future or
pending decision in which you may participate, we could not advise whether you may or
may not have a conflict of interest at this time by virtue of your ownership of Enron stock
and the specific performance of your duties.

The facts you provide, however, indicate that your investment interest in Enron
Corporation is approximately $272.00. Potential conflicts arise as a result of an official's
economic interests. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in the
situation you describe when there is a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more
in a given entity. (§ 87103, subd. (a); Reg. 18703.1, subd. (a).) Because your interest in
Enron at this point is less than $2,000, you do not have an economic interest in Enron.
Because you do not have an economic interest in Enron, ownership of the stock at this
time and at the current value will not give rise to a conflict of interest.

You are encouraged to write the Commission for further advice when and if your
circumstances change. In the meantime, I have enclosed a fact sheet designed to help
public officials determine whether they may have a conflict of interest.

Please note that if your interest in the investment club is worth $2,000 or more,
the club will be considered an economic interest of yours.³ You have not provided
information on the value of your interest in the club, and therefore we do not analyze this
issue.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Meneñaca
General Counsel

By: C. Scott Tocher
Counsel, Legal Division

CST:jg
I:\AdviceLetrs\01-192
Enclosures

³ A business entity is defined as "any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including but
not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or
association." (§ 82005; See also Blunt Advice Letter, No. A-96-094, enclosed.)
